evidence_excerpt: After bladder stone removal and vesicostomy, follow-up showed normal kidneys without hydronephrosis, stable left renal calculi, a completely decompressed bladder, normal eGFR (~120 mL/min/1.73 m2), and proteinuria partially improved with enalapril. No nephrotic syndrome developed.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Ongoing tubular dysfunction with hypomagnesemia, significant proteinuria, recurrent UTIs associated with seizures, multidrug-resistant organisms, and need for open lithotomy and vesicostomy; residual left renal calculi and continued prophylactic management.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: Recurrent UTIs associated with seizures; cultures with multidrug-resistant organisms despite antibiotic prophylaxis, prompting change to methenamine hippurate.
At age 7, imaging revealed a large bladder calculus (3.3 × 3.2 cm) and three non‑obstructing left renal calculi.
Underwent cystoscopy with incomplete laser lithotripsy of the bladder stone (struvite).
Subsequently had open lithotomy to remove the remaining bladder stone and creation of a vesicostomy for low-pressure emptying.
Post‑operative follow-up showed a completely decompressed bladder and normal kidneys without hydronephrosis; left renal calculi remained stable.
Proteinuria partially responded to enalapril 2.5 mg daily.

notes_on_uncertainty: Index time was inferred as the exome-confirmed OSGEP diagnosis; some initial management (magnesium supplementation) preceded this. Exact dates are not provided; ages were mapped to relative periods (mid-term ~1 year; long-term ~>2 years post-diagnosis). Frequency of UTIs after vesicostomy is not explicitly documented, so sustained improvement is inferred from imaging and bladder decompression rather than infection counts.
